examples.title: Sprint Planning Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for Sprint Planning meeting process
examples.code:
SPRINT PLANNING
BEGIN
 INPUT: Product Backlog, Sprint Goal (optional)
 OUTPUT: Sprint Backlog, Sprint Goal
 
 // Part 1: What can be delivered?
 ProductOwner.PresentPrioritizedBacklog()
 Team.EstimateCapacity()
 
 WHILE Team.HasCapacity() AND Backlog.HasItems() DO
 Item = Backlog.SelectTopItem()
 IF Team.CanComplete(Item) THEN
 SprintBacklog.Add(Item)
 Team.DecreaseCapacity(Item.Estimate)
 ELSE
 BREAK
 END IF
 END WHILE
 
 // Part 2: How will it be achieved?
 FOR EACH Item IN SprintBacklog DO
 TaskList = Team Decompose(Item)
 Item.AddTasks(TaskList)
 END FOR
 
 SprintBacklog.Finalize()
 RETURN SprintBacklog, SprintGoal
END
examples.explanation: Sprint Planning is time-boxed (max 8 hours). First part determines WHAT to deliver based on team capacity and backlog priority. Second part determines HOW by breaking items into tasks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = backlog items considered

examples.title: Daily Stand-up Meeting
examples.description: Algorithm for conducting daily Scrum meeting
examples.code:
DAILY STANDUP
BEGIN
 INPUT: Team Members, Sprint Backlog, Previous Day's Work
 OUTPUT: Updated Sprint Status, Impediments List
 
 TIMEBOX = 15 minutes
 StartTimer()
 
 Impediments = Empty List
 
 FOR EACH Member IN Team DO
 // Three questions format
 Yesterday = Member.GetCompletedWork()
 Today = Member.GetPlannedWork()
 Blockers = Member.GetBlockers()
 
 // Share with team
 PRINT: Member.Name + ": " + Yesterday + ", " + Today
 
 // Collect impediments
 IF Blockers.Length > 0 THEN
 Impediments.Add(Blockers)
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Scrum Master addresses impediments
 FOR EACH Impediment IN Impediments DO
 ScrumMaster.Resolve(Impediment)
 END IF
 
 RETURN SprintBacklog, Impediments
END
examples.explanation: Daily Stand-up follows the three questions format: What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? Are there any blockers? Time-boxed to 15 minutes maximum.

examples.title: Sprint Retrospective Process
examples.code:
SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE
BEGIN
 INPUT: Sprint Data, Previous Retrospective Actions
 OUTPUT: Improvement Actions for Next Sprint
 
 // Set the stage
 Team.ReflectOnSprint()
 
 // Gather data (What happened?)
 DataPoints = CollectMetrics()
 DataPoints.Add(TeamExperiences)
 
 // Generate insights (Why did it happen?)
 Insights = Analyze(DataPoints)
 
 // Decide actions (What will we do differently?)
 // Technique: Start-Stop-Continue
 Start = [] // New behaviors to start
 Stop = [] // Behaviors to stop
 Continue = [] // Behaviors to maintain
 
 FOR EACH Insight IN Insights DO
 Action = DetermineAction(Insight)
 Categorize(Action, Start, Stop, Continue)
 END FOR
 
 // Close retrospective
 ActionItems = PrioritizeActions(Start + Stop)
 AssignOwners(ActionItems)
 
 RETURN ActionItems
END
examples.explanation: Sprint Retrospective helps teams continuously improve. Common techniques include Start-Stop-Continue, Mad-Sad-Glad, and 4Ls (Liked, Learned, Lacked, Longed For).

examples.title: Kanban Flow Management
examples.description: Pseudocode for Kanban pull-based system with WIP limits
examples.code:
KANBAN BOARD
BEGIN
 // Initialize columns with WIP limits
 Columns = {
 "To Do": WIP_Limit = 10,
 "In Progress": WIP_Limit = 5,
 "Review": WIP_Limit = 3,
 "Done": WIP_Limit = Infinite
 }
 
 // Pull item when capacity available
 FUNCTION PullItem(Column)
 IF Column.CurrentItems < Column.WIP_Limit THEN
 Item = PreviousColumn.GetNextItem()
 Column.Add(Item)
 RETURN Item
 ELSE
 RETURN NULL // Column at capacity
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Flow items through columns
 WHILE Board.HasItems() DO
 FOR EACH Column IN Columns (except Done) DO
 IF Column.CanPull() THEN
 Item = PullItem(Column)
 IF Item != NULL THEN
 ProcessItem(Item)
 MoveToNextColumn(Item)
 END IF
 END IF
 END IF
 END WHILE
 
 // Calculate metrics
 LeadTime = CalculateAverageTime(ToDo_to_Done)
 CycleTime = CalculateAverageTime(InProgress_to_Done)
 Throughput = ItemsCompleted / TimePeriod
 
 RETURN LeadTime, CycleTime, Throughput
END
examples.explanation: Kanban uses pull-based system where items are pulled into a column only when capacity (WIP limit) is available. This prevents overloading and optimizes flow.

examples.title: Velocity Calculation
examples.description: Algorithm for calculating and using team velocity
examples.code:
VELOCITY CALCULATION
BEGIN
 // Collect completed story points per sprint
 SprintHistory = [Sprint1_Points, Sprint2_Points, ..., SprintN_Points]
 
 // Calculate rolling average velocity
 FUNCTION CalculateVelocity(SprintHistory, Window)
 LastNSprints = SprintHistory.GetLast(Window) // typically 3-5 sprints
 Average = Sum(LastNSprints) / Window
 RETURN Average
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Use velocity for planning
 FUNCTION PlanSprint(AvailableCapacity, Backlog)
 TargetVelocity = CalculateVelocity(SprintHistory, 3)
 SelectedItems = []
 CurrentLoad = 0
 
 FOR EACH Item IN Backlog DO
 IF CurrentLoad + Item.StoryPoints <= TargetVelocity THEN
 SelectedItems.Add(Item)
 CurrentLoad += Item.StoryPoints
 ELSE
 BREAK
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN SelectedItems
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Update history after sprint
 FUNCTION UpdateHistory(CompletedPoints)
 SprintHistory.Add(CompletedPoints)
 IF SprintHistory.Length > 10 THEN
 SprintHistory.RemoveOldest()
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
 
 RETURN Velocity
END
examples.explanation: Velocity is calculated as the average story points completed over the last 3-5 sprints. It helps teams plan future sprints and improve estimation accuracy.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for velocity calc, O(m) for planning
